artists.name: Charley Patton
artists.bio: Один из основателей дельта-блюза, «отец Delta Blues», учитель целого поколения блюзменов Миссисипи.

artists.name: Son House
artists.bio: Проповедник и блюзмен дельты Миссисипи, повлиявший на Роберта Джонсона и Мадди Уотерса.

artists.name: Blind Lemon Jefferson
artists.bio: Пионер техасского кантри-блюза, один из первых коммерчески успешных блюзменов.

artists.name: Robert Johnson
artists.bio: Легенда дельта-блюза, чьи 29 записей определили язык послевоенного блюза и рока.

artists.name: Lead Belly
artists.bio: Фолк- и блюз-музыкант, хранитель американских народных песен, повлиявший на фолк-возрождение.

artists.name: Sister Rosetta Tharpe
artists.bio: «Крёстная мать рок-н-ролла», соединившая госпел с электрогитарой.

artists.name: Arthur «Big Boy» Crudup
artists.bio: Блюзмен дельты, автор «That's All Right», ставшей дебютным синглом Элвиса Пресли.

artists.name: Big Mama Thornton
artists.bio: Исполнительница, первой записавшая «Hound Dog» и «Ball and Chain».

artists.name: T-Bone Walker
artists.bio: Пионер электрического блюза, во многом создавший словарь электрогитары.

artists.name: Muddy Waters
artists.bio: «Отец чикагского блюза», мост между дельта-блюзом и рок-н-роллом.

artists.name: Howlin' Wolf
artists.bio: Мощнейший голос чикагского блюза, ученик Чарли Паттона.

artists.name: B.B. King
artists.bio: «Король блюза», чья выразительная игра определила блюзовую гитару XX века.

artists.name: Bo Diddley
artists.bio: Pioneer of the syncopated 'Bo Diddley beat'. Direct influence on The Rolling Stones, The Who and generations of rock guitarists.

artists.name: John Lee Hooker
artists.bio: Detroit bluesman whose hypnotic boogie rhythm influenced The Animals, The Rolling Stones and virtually every strand of British blues-rock.

artists.name: Sonny Boy Williamson II
artists.bio: Rice Miller — Chicago blues harmonica master who recorded a live album with The Yardbirds in 1963 and directly shaped Clapton and Page.

artists.name: Chuck Berry
artists.bio: Один из создателей рок-н-ролла, автор фирменных гитарных риффов жанра.

artists.name: Little Richard
artists.bio: «Архитектор рок-н-ролла», взрывной вокал и фортепиано которого задали энергию жанра.

artists.name: Elvis Presley
artists.bio: «Король рок-н-ролла», вынесший ритм-энд-блюз в мейнстрим.

artists.name: Buddy Holly
artists.bio: Новатор рок-н-ролла, повлиявший на формат группы и песенное письмо 1960-х.

artists.name: Carl Perkins
artists.bio: Author of 'Blue Suede Shoes' and one of the architects of rockabilly. The Beatles recorded four of his songs and cited him as a primary model.

artists.name: The Everly Brothers
artists.bio: Don and Phil Everly. Their close two-part harmonies became the blueprint for The Beatles, The Beach Boys and Simon & Garfunkel.

artists.name: Eddie Cochran
artists.bio: Rockabilly pioneer behind 'Summertime Blues' and 'C'mon Everybody'. Died at 21; directly influenced The Who and The Kinks.

artists.name: Woody Guthrie
artists.bio: Икона американского фолка, голос рабочего движения, кумир Боба Дилана.

artists.name: Hank Williams
artists.bio: Основоположник современного кантри, повлиявший на рок и сонграйтинг.

artists.name: The Carter Family
artists.bio: The first great family of American country music. A.P., Sara and Maybelle Carter created the canon of mountain music and the first country guitar patterns.

artists.name: Jimmie Rodgers
artists.bio: Father of Country Music. His synthesis of blues, folk and yodeling — the blue yodel — established the emotional vocabulary of American country.

artists.name: Stephen Foster
artists.bio: The first great American popular songwriter. 'Oh! Susanna', 'Beautiful Dreamer' — melodies that became part of the American folk canon, quoted by Charles Ives.

artists.name: The Beatles
artists.bio: Самая влиятельная рок-группа XX века, переопределившая поп- и рок-музыку.

artists.name: The Rolling Stones
artists.bio: Группа, принёсшая чикагский блюз британской и мировой публике.

artists.name: The Beach Boys
artists.bio: The Wilson brothers' California group, evolving from surf-rock to the experimental Pet Sounds and beyond.

artists.name: The Animals
artists.bio: Newcastle group fronted by Eric Burdon — one of the main conduits of American blues into the British Invasion.

artists.name: The Yardbirds
artists.bio: London group through which three legendary guitarists passed: Eric Clapton, Jeff Beck and Jimmy Page.

artists.name: The Doors
artists.bio: Los Angeles group led by poet and singer Jim Morrison. Psychedelic rock with a literary and theatrical dimension.

artists.name: The Who
artists.bio: Townshend, Daltrey, Entwistle and Moon — pioneers of the rock opera and the three-chord anthem.

artists.name: The Kinks
artists.bio: Ray Davies's London group combining hard rock with English music-hall tradition and social irony.

artists.name: Bob Dylan
artists.bio: Поэт и музыкант, соединивший фолк с роком и изменивший роль текста в песне.

artists.name: Jimi Hendrix
artists.bio: Революционер электрогитары, переопределивший её звучание и технику.

artists.name: Janis Joplin
artists.bio: Texas singer with extraordinary range and unbridled expressiveness. Voice of the Woodstock generation.

artists.name: Simon & Garfunkel
artists.bio: Paul Simon and Art Garfunkel. Refined vocal harmonies and Simon's poetic lyrics — the peak of 1960s folk-pop.

artists.name: Joan Baez
artists.bio: Queen of Folk. Her pure soprano voice and activism made her a symbol of the civil rights movement.

artists.name: Donovan
artists.bio: Scottish singer-songwriter and British answer to Dylan. Merged folk, psychedelia and Eastern influences.

artists.name: Buffalo Springfield
artists.bio: Short-lived supergroup: Neil Young, Stephen Stills, Richie Furay. Forerunner of country-rock and CSNY.

artists.name: The Byrds
artists.bio: Los Angeles band that invented folk-rock. Roger McGuinn's 12-string Rickenbacker and three-part harmonies.

artists.name: Bessie Smith
artists.bio: Empress of the Blues. Powerful contralto, unbridled emotional depth and commanding stage presence made her the defining voice of classic blues.

artists.name: Odetta
artists.bio: Singer and guitarist, the 'voice of the civil rights movement'. Her powerful folk blues rooted in the African-American tradition brought it to concert halls and college campuses.

artists.name: Pete Seeger
artists.bio: Patriarch of the American folk revival. Banjo player, singer and activist who made the protest song a primary vehicle of political art.

artists.name: Wanda Jackson
artists.bio: Queen of Rockabilly. One of the first women in rock'n'roll, she fused country sweetness with raw, unrestrained energy before the genre had a name.

artists.name: Ma Rainey
artists.bio: Mother of the Blues. First great blues singer; through Bessie Smith she shaped all vocal blues.

artists.name: George Harrison
artists.bio: The Quiet Beatle. His 12-string sound and Indian philosophy became part of The Byrds' DNA.

artists.name: Ravi Shankar
artists.bio: The supreme master of the sitar and ambassador of Hindustani classical music to the world. His concerts and recordings in the 1960s brought ragas to Western ears.

artists.name: Cream
artists.bio: Первое рок-супертрио, соединившее блюз с виртуозной импровизацией.

artists.name: The Stooges
artists.bio: Прото-панк-группа Игги Попа, предвосхитившая панк-рок.

artists.name: Deep Purple
artists.bio: Пионеры хард-рока и хэви-метала.

artists.name: Black Sabbath
artists.bio: Основоположники хэви-метала.

artists.name: Led Zeppelin
artists.bio: Группа, превратившая блюз-рок в мощный хард-рок и заложившая каноны жанра.

artists.name: Motörhead
artists.bio: Группа Лемми Килмистера, мост между панком и спид-металом.

artists.name: Diamond Head
artists.bio: Группа новой волны британского хэви-метала, ключевое влияние на трэш.

artists.name: Metallica
artists.bio: Крупнейшая трэш-метал-группа, вынесшая жанр в мейнстрим.

artists.name: Ramones
artists.bio: Группа, задавшая шаблон панк-рока — быстро, коротко, мелодично.

artists.name: Sex Pistols
artists.bio: Символ британского панк-движения.

artists.name: New York Dolls
artists.bio: Нью-йоркская протопанк-группа в макияже и каблуках. Сырая энергия и андрогинность — прямое влияние на панк и постпанк.

artists.name: Patti Smith
artists.bio: «Крёстная мать панка». Horses (1975) — альбом, показавший, что рок может быть литературным высказыванием: поэзия Рембо и энергия панка в одном флаконе.

artists.name: Black Flag
artists.bio: Пионеры хардкор-панка и DIY-сцены.

artists.name: Pixies
artists.bio: Группа, чья динамика «тихо-громко» определила альтернативный рок 1990-х.

artists.name: Nirvana
artists.bio: Группа, выведшая гранж и альтернативу в мейнстрим.

artists.name: Stevie Ray Vaughan
artists.bio: Гитарист, возродивший интерес к блюзу в 1980-х.

artists.name: Fannie Charles Dillon
artists.bio: American composer, pianist and educator known for transcribing bird calls into musical scores. Taught at Los Angeles High School in the 1920s, pioneering natural sound as a source of musical material.

artists.name: John Cage
artists.bio: American composer whose prepared piano, chance operations (aleatoric music) and 4'33" redefined the boundaries of music — a foundational figure for every strand of experimental and electronic art.

artists.name: Karlheinz Stockhausen
artists.bio: Композитор-авангардист, пионер электронной и алеаторической музыки, повлиявший на рок и электронику.

artists.name: Nina Simone
artists.bio: Певица, пианистка и активистка, соединившая классику, джаз, госпел и соул в уникальную манеру.

artists.name: The Velvet Underground
artists.bio: Группа Лу Рида и Джона Кейла, чей авангардный рок стал фундаментом арт-рока, панка и инди.

artists.name: David Bowie
artists.bio: Хамелеон рок-музыки, соединивший арт-рок, глэм и электронику и переопределивший образ артиста.

artists.name: Brian Eno
artists.bio: Музыкант и продюсер, основатель эмбиента, участник Roxy Music, архитектор студийного звука.

artists.name: Kate Bush
artists.bio: Певица и автор, новатор арт-попа, соединившая литературный текст, театральность и студийные эксперименты.

artists.name: Cabaret Voltaire
artists.bio: Шеффилдская группа, пионеры индустриальной музыки и раннего электронного эксперимента.

artists.name: Genesis P-Orridge
artists.bio: Основатель Throbbing Gristle и Psychic TV, идеолог индустриальной музыки и перформанса.

artists.name: The Four Freshmen
artists.bio: Indiana vocal jazz quartet whose innovatively arranged four-part close harmonies set a new standard for vocal sophistication in American popular music.

artists.name: Phil Spector
artists.bio: Creator of the Wall of Sound — a dense, reverb-saturated production technique that redefined recorded pop in the early 1960s.

artists.name: Mose Allison
artists.bio: Jazz-blues pianist and songwriter from Mississippi. His wry, sardonic delivery and economical piano style made him a singular voice bridging jazz, blues and wit.

artists.name: King Oliver
artists.bio: New Orleans cornet king and mentor of Louis Armstrong, whose Creole Jazz Band set the template for Chicago jazz.

artists.name: Duke Ellington
artists.bio: Composer, pianist and bandleader whose half-century career made him the defining figure of American jazz.

artists.name: Louis Armstrong
artists.bio: Satchmo — the first great jazz soloist and global ambassador of jazz, who transformed improvisation and trumpet technique.

artists.name: Django Reinhardt
artists.bio: Romani-Belgian guitarist who co-created Gypsy jazz and remains one of the most singular voices in the history of guitar.

artists.name: Cab Calloway
artists.bio: Bandleader and singer whose flamboyant showmanship and scat singing made him one of the great entertainers of the swing era.

artists.name: Hoagy Carmichael
artists.bio: Composer of 'Stardust' and 'Georgia on My Mind'. One of the most influential American popular song composers of the 20th century.

artists.name: George Formby
artists.bio: Lancashire entertainer and virtuoso banjolele player. One of Britain's biggest pre-war stars, known for comic songs, cheeky wit and remarkable fretboard dexterity.

artists.name: Lonnie Donegan
artists.bio: King of Skiffle. His stripped-down, do-it-yourself approach — acoustic guitar, washboard, homemade bass — sparked a wave of amateur musicianship across Britain in the late 1950s.

artists.name: Slim Whitman
artists.bio: American country singer known for his pure falsetto voice and yodeling style. His gentle, romantic delivery made him one of the most internationally successful country artists of the postwar era.

artists.name: Buddy Bolden
artists.bio: The first king of New Orleans jazz. No recordings survive, but Armstrong and Jelly Roll Morton cited Bolden as the originator of the jazz style.

artists.name: Scott Joplin
artists.bio: King of Ragtime. 'Maple Leaf Rag' and 'The Entertainer' defined a genre that bridged African-American vernacular music and the European concert tradition.

artists.name: James P. Johnson
artists.bio: Father of Harlem stride piano. His virtuosic left-hand bass patterns and swinging right-hand melodies set the technical and stylistic standard for the jazz piano of the 1920s.

artists.name: Coleman Hawkins
artists.bio: Father of the jazz tenor saxophone. 'Body and Soul' (1939) — the first great jazz improvisation on record. Bridge between swing and bebop.

artists.name: Ludwig van Beethoven
artists.bio: The pivotal figure between Classical and Romantic eras. His nine symphonies, string quartets and piano sonatas established the structural logic that defined European concert music for a century.

artists.name: Johannes Brahms
artists.bio: Master of late Romantic counterpoint and symphonic form. His dense motivic development and advanced harmonic language pushed tonal music to its structural limits.

artists.name: Richard Wagner
artists.bio: Composer, conductor and theorist whose music dramas transformed 19th-century opera. The extreme chromaticism of Tristan und Isolde pushed Western harmony to the edge of tonality.

artists.name: Franz Liszt
artists.bio: Hungarian pianist-composer who redefined virtuosity and Romantic expression. His late works explored increasingly chromatic and experimental harmonic language.

artists.name: Claude Debussy
artists.bio: Father of musical Impressionism. His use of whole-tone scales, modal harmony and timbral color dissolved the rigid structures of German Romanticism and opened new sonic worlds.

artists.name: Nikolai Rimsky-Korsakov
artists.bio: Russian master of orchestration and member of The Five. His vivid orchestral colors and mastery of folk-derived melody made him the defining voice of Russian nationalist music.

artists.name: George Gershwin
artists.bio: The great American synthesis: jazz + classical. Rhapsody in Blue and Porgy and Bess bridge vernacular and art music.

artists.name: Igor Stravinsky
artists.bio: The greatest revolutionary in 20th-century classical music. The Rite of Spring (1913) — a rhythmic explosion that influenced jazz, rock and all popular music.

artists.name: Arnold Schoenberg
artists.bio: Creator of atonal and serial technique. His twelve-tone method — in which all notes of the chromatic scale are treated as equal — broke the centuries-old system of tonal harmony.

artists.name: Edgard Varèse
artists.bio: French-American composer who rejected melody and harmony in favour of organised sound. His works for percussion and early electronic instruments made him a founding figure of the sonic avant-garde.

artists.name: Henry Cowell
artists.bio: American avant-gardist who invented tone clusters and string piano technique. His New Musical Resources (1930) proposed rhythmic and tonal systems far ahead of their time.

artists.name: Charles Ives
artists.bio: Father of the American musical avant-garde. His symphonies and sonatas employed polyrhythm, polytonality and musical collage decades before European modernists explored similar territory.

artists.name: Béla Bartók
artists.bio: Hungarian composer and ethnomusicologist. Synthesis of Central European folk music with academic modernism — forerunner of world music and ethnic integration in rock.

artists.name: Jackie Wilson
artists.bio: R&B showman nicknamed 'Mr. Excitement'. His acrobatic stage performances, vocal range and ability to move an audience set a new standard for the live soul act.

artists.name: James Brown
artists.bio: The Godfather of Soul and Father of Funk, whose explosive stage presence and rhythmic innovations defined soul, funk and hip-hop.

artists.name: Curtis Mayfield
artists.bio: Soul and funk visionary whose socially charged music with The Impressions and his solo career influenced generations of artists.

artists.name: Marvin Gaye
artists.bio: Motown star who evolved from pop hits to socially conscious albums, redefining what soul music could say and feel.

artists.name: Ray Charles
artists.bio: The Father of Soul. His synthesis of gospel fervour and R&B rhythm — born out of his blindness, his piano mastery and an extraordinary voice — reshaped American popular music.

artists.name: Michael Jackson
artists.bio: The King of Pop, whose synthesis of R&B, soul and pop combined with revolutionary dance defined modern pop music globally.

artists.name: George Clinton
artists.bio: Architect of P-Funk (Parliament-Funkadelic). Psychedelic funk and carnival showmanship — a model for acid house culture.

artists.name: Sam Cooke
artists.bio: The Father of Soul. His vocal beauty, songwriting craft and independence from labels were the model for the entire Motown generation.

artists.name: Billie Holiday
artists.bio: Lady Day. Her behind-the-beat phrasing, microtonal inflections and ability to transform any song into autobiography made her the most personal voice in jazz history.

artists.name: Nat King Cole
artists.bio: Pianist and singer who defined elegant jazz-pop crossover. His velvet baritone, impeccable phrasing and piano trio recordings set a standard of sophistication in mid-century American song.

artists.name: Clara Ward
artists.bio: One of the most powerful gospel singers of the 20th century. Her dramatic, full-voiced delivery and theatrical stage presence elevated gospel into a high art form.

artists.name: The Impressions
artists.bio: Curtis Mayfield's Chicago group. Refined harmonies and socially conscious lyrics — a direct influence on Motown vocal groups.

artists.name: The Moonglows
artists.bio: One of the definitive doo-wop groups of the 1950s. Their close harmonies, gospel undertones and Harvey Fuqua's lead voice made them a touchstone of the Chicago R&B scene.

artists.name: Stevie Wonder
artists.bio: Motown child prodigy who became one of the greatest singer-songwriters of the 20th century — from early hits to Songs in the Key of Life.

artists.name: Aretha Franklin
artists.bio: The Queen of Soul. Her voice, technique and power represent the absolute peak of American vocal art.

artists.name: Otis Redding
artists.bio: The King of Soul. Died at 26 leaving an indelible mark — from 'Respect' to '(Sittin' On) The Dock of the Bay'.

artists.name: The Supremes
artists.bio: Motown's premier female group. Diana Ross, Florence Ballard and Mary Wilson. Twelve US number-one hits.

artists.name: The Temptations
artists.bio: Motown's definitive male vocal group, evolving from classic soul to psychedelic 'Cloud Nine' and 'Papa Was a Rollin' Stone'.

artists.name: Four Tops
artists.bio: Detroit quartet led by Levi Stubbs. 'I Can't Help Myself' and 'Reach Out I'll Be There' — peaks of Motown soul-pop.

artists.name: Smokey Robinson & the Miracles
artists.bio: William 'Smokey' Robinson — Motown's poet. Bob Dylan called him 'the greatest living poet in America'.

artists.name: Booker T. & the M.G.'s
artists.bio: Stax Records' house band. Booker T. Jones, Steve Cropper, Duck Dunn and Al Jackson Jr. 'Green Onions' is one of the most recognisable instrumental riffs in rock history.

artists.name: The Band
artists.bio: Robbie Robertson and Levon Helm's Canadian-American group. Music from Big Pink (1968) reinterpreted the American folk tradition.

artists.name: Santana
artists.bio: Carlos Santana's San Francisco group. A revolutionary fusion of Latin rhythm, blues and psychedelia — Woodstock 1969.

artists.name: MC5
artists.bio: Wayne Kramer and Fred 'Sonic' Smith's Detroit group. Kick Out the Jams — one of the most ferocious rock albums ever made and a direct punk precursor.

artists.name: Blue Cheer
artists.bio: San Francisco group regarded as proto-metal pioneers. Their 1968 cover of 'Summertime Blues' was one of the first proto-metal recordings.

artists.name: The Seeds
artists.bio: Sky Saxon's Los Angeles garage group. 'Pushin' Too Hard' is a canonical example of American garage-rock.

artists.name: The Sonics
artists.bio: Tacoma group — absolute pioneers of garage-rock. Raw sound, screaming vocals and distortion years before punk.

artists.name: Tito Puente
artists.bio: The King of Mambo. His volcanic timbales technique, showmanship and fusion of Afro-Cuban rhythms with jazz made him the defining figure of Latin music in New York.

artists.name: Lightnin' Hopkins
artists.bio: Texas bluesman whose free, conversational guitar style and ability to stretch a single phrase across time made him one of the most distinctive voices in post-war blues.

artists.name: Sun Ra
artists.bio: Cosmic jazz avant-gardist and bandleader who built a mythology around Saturn, outer space and Afrofuturism. His orchestras explored free jazz, electronic instruments and collective improvisation.

artists.name: Peter Gabriel
artists.bio: Genesis frontman and solo artist. His theatrical narrative ambition, use of masks and costume, and willingness to fuse storytelling with progressive rock redefined the concept of the rock performer.

artists.name: Pink Floyd
artists.bio: Syd Barrett's, then Roger Waters's and David Gilmour's London group. The Dark Side of the Moon and The Wall.

artists.name: John Coltrane
artists.bio: Saxophonist who expanded jazz to cosmic dimensions. A Love Supreme and Giant Steps influenced psychedelic rock's improvisational approach.

artists.name: Miles Davis
artists.bio: The greatest jazz innovator of the 20th century. Kind of Blue and Bitches Brew shaped psychedelic and progressive rock.

artists.name: La Monte Young
artists.bio: Father of minimalist music and drone aesthetics. Through John Cale, directly shaped the sound of The Velvet Underground.

artists.name: Clarence 'Frogman' Henry
artists.bio: New Orleans singer and pianist. His 'Ain't Got No Home' was one of John Fogerty's early reference points.

artists.name: Arthur Alexander
artists.bio: Alabama singer who fused country and soul. His songs were recorded by both The Beatles and The Rolling Stones.

artists.name: Solomon Burke
artists.bio: King of Rock 'n' Soul. His powerful voice and gospel foundation inspired The Rascals and the wider soul movement.

artists.name: Johann Sebastian Bach
artists.bio: The supreme Baroque composer. His counterpoint and organ fugues became the foundation for baroque-pop and progressive rock.

artists.name: Jefferson Airplane
artists.bio: San Francisco's flagship psychedelic band of the Haight-Ashbury scene. Grace Slick, Marty Balin, Paul Kantner.

artists.name: Grateful Dead
artists.bio: Jerry Garcia's San Francisco band. Pioneers of improvisational rock and the live concert culture.

artists.name: Creedence Clearwater Revival
artists.bio: John Fogerty's California band. Swamp rock rooted in blues and country — the antithesis of psychedelia.

artists.name: Love
artists.bio: Arthur Lee's Los Angeles band. Forever Changes (1967) is one of the greatest albums of the psychedelic era.

artists.name: The Zombies
artists.bio: Rod Argent and Colin Blunstone's British band. Odessey and Oracle (1968) is the peak of baroque pop.

artists.name: Small Faces
artists.bio: Steve Marriott and Ronni Lane's London mod band. From R&B to the psychedelia of Ogden's Nut Gone Flake.

artists.name: Traffic
artists.bio: Steve Winwood and Dave Mason's band. Merged rock, jazz and folk into a mature progressive sound.

artists.name: Procol Harum
artists.bio: Gary Brooker's London band. 'A Whiter Shade of Pale' — built on Bach and blues, one of rock's most iconic singles.

artists.name: The Spencer Davis Group
artists.bio: Birmingham band that made Steve Winwood a star. 'Keep On Running' and 'Gimme Some Lovin'' are British soul-rock benchmarks.

artists.name: The Hollies
artists.bio: Manchester band with exceptional three-part harmonies. Allan Clarke, Graham Nash and Tony Hicks.

artists.name: The Monkees
artists.bio: The TV band that became real. Davy Jones, Micky Dolenz, Peter Tork, Mike Nesmith.

artists.name: The Mamas & the Papas
artists.bio: Los Angeles group with lush four-part harmonies. John and Michelle Phillips, Cass Elliot, Denny Doherty.

artists.name: The Rascals
artists.bio: Felix Cavaliere's New York band. Blue-eyed soul at its finest — 'Good Lovin'', 'Groovin'', 'People Got to Be Free'.

artists.name: The Turtles
artists.bio: Los Angeles band known for 'Happy Together'. One of the symbols of California pop in the 1960s.

artists.name: Sly and the Family Stone
artists.bio: Sly Stone's San Francisco band. Pioneers of psychedelic funk who radically mixed races and genres.

artists.name: Bob Marley
artists.bio: The face of reggae worldwide, who fused Jamaican roots music with soul and Rastafari spirituality to reach audiences across generations.

artists.name: Kraftwerk
artists.bio: Düsseldorf synthesizer pioneers who invented the template for electronic pop, influencing virtually every strand of modern electronic music.

artists.name: The Smiths
artists.bio: Morrissey and Johnny Marr's Manchester band. Literary melancholy and chiming guitar redefined British indie rock in the 1980s.

artists.name: Happy Mondays
artists.bio: Shaun Ryder's Manchester band. Fused rock guitars with dance beats and acid-house culture — the founders of Madchester.

artists.name: Joy Division
artists.bio: Mancunian post-punk band whose bleak atmosphere and Ian Curtis's commanding presence defined the sound of late-1970s alternative music.

artists.name: New Order
artists.bio: Formed from the ashes of Joy Division, New Order fused post-punk with electronic dance music, pioneering the bridge between indie and club culture.

artists.name: Marc Bolan
artists.bio: Singer, songwriter and glam-rock icon. T. Rex combined electric boogie, cosmic poetry and androgynous image into one of the defining sounds of early 1970s British rock.

artists.name: Queen
artists.bio: Maximalist British rock band whose operatic grandeur, layered arrangements and showmanship became the stadium-rock blueprint.

artists.name: Alexander Scriabin
artists.bio: Русский композитор-мистик. От шопеновского романтизма к собственной «мистической» гармонии и идее синтеза музыки, цвета и света в «Прометее» с партией света.

artists.name: Pierre Schaeffer
artists.bio: Французский инженер и композитор. Изобретатель musique concrète — записанные и обработанные реальные звуки как основа композиции. Основатель студии GRM.

artists.name: Arseny Avraamov
artists.bio: Советский композитор-радикал. «Симфония гудков» (1922) — индустриальная симфония из заводских сирен и пушек. Пионер микротональности и звукового авангарда в России.

artists.name: Modest Mussorgsky
artists.bio: Русский композитор — участник «Могучей кучки». Опера «Борис Годунов» и «Картинки с выставки» отличаются смелой гармонией, речитативной вокальной декламацией и национальным колоритом.

artists.name: Frédéric Chopin
artists.bio: Польский композитор и пианист. Создал язык романтического фортепиано — ноктюрны, прелюдии, этюды, мазурки. Его хроматизм и педальные эффекты стали фундаментом для последующих поколений.

artists.name: Luigi Russolo
artists.bio: Итальянский художник и композитор-футурист. Манифест «Искусство шумов» (1913) и изобретение «шумовых машин» (intonarumori) заложили концептуальную основу шумовой музыки XX века.

artists.name: Olivier Messiaen
artists.bio: Французский композитор и органист. Квартет на конец времени, Turangalîla-Symphony. Синтез тотального сериализма, индийских ритмов и католической мистики — важнейший педагог авангарда.

artists.name: Anton Webern
artists.bio: Австрийский композитор — ученик Шёнберга. Предельная концентрация, пуантилизм, полная додекафония. Его принцип структурной краткости и сериализм стали моделью для послевоенного авангарда.

artists.name: Funkadelic
artists.bio: Детройтская группа Джорджа Клинтона — психоделическое крыло проекта Parliament-Funkadelic. Maggot Brain (1971) соединил тяжёлую гитару, фанк-грув и психоделический космизм.

artists.name: The Mothers of Invention
artists.bio: Группа Фрэнка Заппы. Freak Out! (1966) соединил авангард, сатиру и рок — концептуальная амбициозность без жанровых границ.

artists.name: Avenged Sevenfold
artists.bio: Группа из Хантингтон-Бич, Калифорния. Начинали в металкоре, эволюционировали к мелодичному хэви-металу с симфоническими и прогрессивными элементами.

artists.name: Iron Maiden
artists.bio: Лондонская группа, флагман новой волны британского хэви-метала. Стив Харрис, Брюс Дикинсон. Сложные композиции, исторические и литературные темы, виртуозная игра на бас-гитаре.

artists.name: Guns N' Roses
artists.bio: Лос-анджелесская группа Эксла Роуза и Слэша. Appetite for Destruction (1987) — один из самых продаваемых дебютных альбомов в истории рока.

artists.name: Slayer
artists.bio: Калифорнийская группа, часть «большой четвёрки» трэш-метала. Том Арайя, Керри Кинг, Джефф Ханнеман. Reign in Blood — один из самых интенсивных и технически сложных альбомов жанра.

artists.name: Nine Inch Nails
artists.bio: Проект Трента Резнора. The Downward Spiral — веха индастриал-рока, соединяющая электронику, шум и интроспективную лирику.

artists.name: Rage Against the Machine
artists.bio: Лос-анджелесская группа Зака де ла Роча и Тома Морелло. Соединили рэп, фанк и тяжёлый рок с политическим радикализмом текстов.

artists.name: Deftones
artists.bio: Сакраментская группа Чино Морено. Соединили тяжёлый альтернативный метал с атмосферной, почти шугейзовой текстурой звука.

influences.description: Armstrong named Buddy Bolden the first jazz musician he ever heard as a child in New Orleans.

sources.citation: Buddy Bolden was the first jazz musician I ever heard. He was king of the cornet in New Orleans.
sources.author: Louis Armstrong

influences.description: Ellington called Armstrong 'the most original musician' and learned freedom of solo improvisation from him.

sources.citation: Louis Armstrong is the most original musician I have ever known. He taught us all about freedom in music.
sources.author: Duke Ellington

influences.description: Joplin's ragtime was the piano foundation Ellington grew up on as a child.

sources.citation: Scott Joplin's ragtime was the music I heard as a child learning piano. It was the beginning.
sources.author: Duke Ellington

influences.description: James P. Johnson was Ellington's teacher in Harlem stride piano.

sources.citation: James P. Johnson was the master of Harlem stride piano and my teacher in that tradition.
sources.author: Duke Ellington

influences.description: Armstrong showed Hawkins how to build a solo improvisation with architectural logic and lyrical freedom.

sources.citation: Louis Armstrong's approach to improvisation was the primary model for Coleman Hawkins' melodic development.
sources.author: Grove Music Online

influences.description: Hawkins's 'Body and Soul' was one of the first recordings Coltrane mastered — a primary influence on his saxophone approach.

sources.citation: Coleman Hawkins' Body and Soul was one of the first recordings John Coltrane mastered and a primary influence on his saxophone approach.
sources.author: Grove Music Online

influences.description: Ma Rainey was Bessie Smith's early mentor, taking her on tour and teaching her the classic blues tradition.

sources.citation: Ma Rainey was Bessie Smith's early mentor, taking her on tour and teaching her the classic blues tradition.
sources.author: Grove Music Online

influences.description: The Carters and Rodgers recorded at the same Bristol Sessions in August 1927 — the founding moment of country music.

sources.citation: The Carter Family and Jimmie Rodgers both recorded at the Bristol Sessions in August 1927, the founding moment of country music.
sources.author: Country Music Hall of Fame

influences.description: Rodgers and The Carters were the twin pillars of the Bristol Sessions — mutually defining the country canon.

sources.citation: Jimmie Rodgers and The Carter Family recorded at the same Bristol Sessions in 1927, the birth of country music.
sources.author: Country Music Hall of Fame

influences.description: The Carter Family's recordings were foundational for Hank Williams, who grew up listening to them on the radio.

sources.citation: The Carter Family's recordings were foundational for Hank Williams, who grew up listening to them on the radio.
sources.author: Britannica

influences.description: Jimmie Rodgers was Hank Williams' primary model — the blue yodel and autobiographical honesty Williams built upon.

sources.citation: Jimmie Rodgers was Hank Williams' primary model — the blue yodel and autobiographical honesty Williams built upon.
sources.author: Britannica

influences.description: The Carter Family was part of the folk canon Dylan absorbed early in his career — real American music, simple, deep, honest.

sources.citation: The Carter Family was the real American music. Simple, deep, and honest.
sources.author: Bob Dylan

influences.description: Ma Rainey's classic blues tradition was a formative influence on Tharpe's expressive style.

sources.citation: Ma Rainey's classic blues tradition was a formative influence on Sister Rosetta Tharpe's expressive style.
sources.author: Grove Music Online

influences.description: Tharpe's aggressive electric guitar in a gospel context was a direct model for Chuck Berry.

sources.citation: Sister Rosetta Tharpe's aggressive electric guitar in a gospel context was a direct influence on Chuck Berry's style.
sources.author: Grove Music Online

influences.description: Joplin's ragtime was the foundation from which Gershwin synthesised his jazz-classical language.

sources.citation: Scott Joplin's ragtime was a foundational influence on Gershwin's synthesis of jazz and classical traditions.
sources.author: Britannica

influences.description: Gershwin and Ellington were mutual influences working to bridge jazz and classical music in the 1920s.

sources.citation: Gershwin and Ellington were mutual influences, both working to bridge jazz and classical music in the 1920s.
sources.author: Grove Music Online

influences.description: Rimsky-Korsakov was Stravinsky's teacher — the art of orchestration passed directly from him.

sources.citation: Rimsky-Korsakov was my teacher. From him I learned the art of orchestration.
sources.author: Igor Stravinsky

influences.description: Debussy was the decisive influence on the harmonic language of early Stravinsky.

sources.citation: Debussy's harmony was a revelation to me. It opened up a world of color I had not imagined.
sources.author: Igor Stravinsky

influences.description: McCartney named The Rite of Spring as one of the primary non-linear influences on Sgt. Pepper.

sources.citation: Stravinsky's Rite of Spring was one of those records that blew your mind. We listened to it a lot around Sgt. Pepper.
sources.author: Paul McCartney

influences.description: Schoenberg wrote 'Brahms the Progressive' — Brahms's dense counterpoint was a key structural influence on his atonality.

sources.citation: Schoenberg wrote his famous essay Brahms the Progressive, acknowledging Brahms as a key structural influence.
sources.author: Grove Music Online

influences.description: Wagner's extreme chromaticism in Tristan und Isolde was the direct precursor to Schoenberg's atonality.

sources.citation: Wagner's extreme chromaticism in Tristan und Isolde was the direct precursor to Schoenberg's atonality.
sources.author: Britannica

influences.description: John Cale studied Schoenberg's twelve-tone method and brought those ideas directly into The Velvet Underground.

sources.citation: I studied Schoenberg's twelve-tone method and brought those ideas into The Velvet Underground.
sources.author: John Cale

influences.description: Stravinsky's rhythmic innovations in The Rite of Spring were a crucial influence on Varèse's percussive approach.

sources.citation: Stravinsky's rhythmic innovations in The Rite of Spring were a crucial influence on Varèse's percussive approach.
sources.author: Grove Music Online

influences.description: Debussy was a personal mentor to the young Varèse and opened him to the world of timbral expression.

sources.citation: Debussy was a personal mentor to the young Varèse and opened him to the world of timbral expression.
sources.author: Britannica

influences.description: Charles Ives was Cowell's primary mentor and the model for his American experimental thinking.

sources.citation: Charles Ives was Henry Cowell's primary mentor and the model for his American experimental approach.
sources.author: Grove Music Online

influences.description: Bartók's integration of folk music into modernism was a key model for Cowell's approach to American vernacular traditions.

sources.citation: Béla Bartók's integration of folk music into modernism was a key model for Henry Cowell's own approach to American vernacular traditions.
sources.author: Grove Music Online

influences.description: Henry Cowell was John Cage's direct teacher — he introduced Cage to non-Western music, extended piano techniques and American experimentalism.

sources.citation: Henry Cowell was John Cage's primary teacher, introducing him to non-Western music, extended piano techniques and the American experimental tradition.
sources.author: Grove Music Online

influences.description: Stephen Foster's American folk melodies appear throughout Ives's work as quotation and transformation.

sources.citation: Stephen Foster's American folk melodies appear throughout Ives' work as quotation and transformation.
sources.author: Britannica

influences.description: Beethoven's formal structures were the classical model Ives engaged with, transformed and subverted.

sources.citation: Beethoven's formal structures were the classical model Ives engaged with, transformed, and subverted.
sources.author: Grove Music Online

influences.description: Liszt was Bartók's Hungarian predecessor and an early pianistic model in his formation.

sources.citation: Franz Liszt was Bartók's Hungarian predecessor and an early pianistic model in his formation.
sources.author: Grove Music Online

influences.description: 'It was Debussy's music which first showed me the possibility of liberating myself from the tyranny of the major-minor key system.' — Bartók's own words.

sources.citation: It was Debussy's music which first showed me the possibility of liberating myself from the tyranny of the major-minor key system.
sources.author: Béla Bartók

influences.description: Muddy Waters was Hendrix's primary blues foundation — 'I dig Muddy Waters. That's my music, that's the blues.'

sources.citation: I dig Muddy Waters. That's my music, that's the blues.
sources.author: Jimi Hendrix

influences.description: B.B. King was the expressive guitar master Hendrix studied during his early club period.

sources.citation: B.B. King taught me everything I know about the blues. He is the master.
sources.author: Jimi Hendrix

influences.description: Robert Johnson was the spiritual predecessor Hendrix acknowledged as foundational.

sources.citation: Hendrix cited Robert Johnson as a foundational influence on his approach to the blues.
sources.author: AllMusic

influences.description: T-Bone Walker pioneered the showmanship — playing behind his back and with his teeth — that Hendrix made iconic.

sources.citation: T-Bone Walker pioneered the showmanship — playing behind his back and with his teeth — that Hendrix would make iconic.
sources.author: AllMusic

influences.description: Lightnin' Hopkins was among the first blues artists Hendrix absorbed while growing up.

sources.citation: Lightnin' Hopkins was among the first blues artists Hendrix absorbed growing up.
sources.author: AllMusic

influences.description: Hendrix toured as guitarist with Little Richard in 1964–65 — direct school of rock'n'roll showmanship.

sources.citation: Jimi Hendrix toured as guitarist with Little Richard in 1964–1965, before launching his own career.
sources.author: Rock and Roll Hall of Fame

influences.description: Chuck Berry's guitar style was the rock'n'roll foundation Hendrix built upon and then transcended.

sources.citation: Chuck Berry's guitar style was the foundation Hendrix built upon and then transcended.
sources.author: Britannica

influences.description: Bessie Smith was Joplin's primary vocal model; Janis paid for Bessie's gravestone in 1970.

sources.citation: Janis Joplin paid for Bessie Smith's gravestone in 1970, calling her the greatest influence on her singing.
sources.author: Rock and Roll Hall of Fame

influences.description: Ma Rainey is the root of the classic blues vocal tradition Joplin drew from through Bessie Smith.

sources.citation: Joplin drew from the classic blues tradition running from Ma Rainey through Bessie Smith.
sources.author: AllMusic

influences.description: Odetta was Joplin's primary folk-blues model at the start of her career.

sources.citation: Joplin began her career in the folk and blues tradition, with Odetta as a primary early model.
sources.author: AllMusic

influences.description: Wanda Jackson showed that a woman could sing rock with the same raw energy as any man.

sources.citation: Wanda Jackson's raw rockabilly energy was a formative influence on Joplin's vocal approach.
sources.author: AllMusic

influences.description: Ray Charles was a key influence on Joplin, who absorbed his gospel-blues synthesis.

sources.citation: Ray Charles was a key influence on Joplin, who absorbed his gospel-blues synthesis.
sources.author: Britannica

influences.description: Guthrie was Dylan's guiding star — arriving in New York, Dylan's first act was to visit the ailing Guthrie.

sources.citation: Woody Guthrie was my guiding star. I came to New York to see him.
sources.author: Bob Dylan

influences.description: Seeger introduced Dylan to the Greenwich Village folk community and personally championed his early career.

sources.citation: Pete Seeger was the one who really took me under his wing in the folk world.
sources.author: Bob Dylan

influences.description: Lead Belly was one of the first sounds that struck Dylan: 'Those songs went straight through me.'

sources.citation: Lead Belly was the first folk singer I ever heard. Those songs went straight through me.
sources.author: Bob Dylan

influences.description: Williams taught Dylan autobiographical directness — a song as a direct expression of your own life.

sources.citation: Hank Williams showed me that a song could be a direct expression of your own life.
sources.author: Bob Dylan

influences.description: Odetta's recording made Dylan trade his electric guitar for an acoustic on the spot.

sources.citation: When I first heard Odetta, I thought, this is it. I traded my electric guitar for an acoustic right away.
sources.author: Bob Dylan

influences.description: Dylan called Robert Johnson 'the most important blues singer that ever lived'.

sources.citation: Robert Johnson is the most important blues singer that ever lived.
sources.author: Bob Dylan

influences.description: The Everly Brothers' two-part harmonies were the direct model for Simon and Garfunkel.

sources.citation: The Everly Brothers were the model for our harmonies. We spent years trying to sound like them.
sources.author: Paul Simon

influences.description: Dylan showed Paul Simon that serious poetry could exist in the form of a pop song.

sources.citation: Bob Dylan showed me that you could write poetry in a pop song and be taken seriously.
sources.author: Paul Simon

influences.description: Baez opened the audience for serious folk and created the space Simon & Garfunkel could inhabit.

sources.citation: Joan Baez paved the way for the folk revival that gave Simon & Garfunkel their audience.
sources.author: AllMusic

influences.description: Guthrie's folk tradition was the soil in which the revival — and Simon & Garfunkel — grew.

sources.citation: Woody Guthrie's tradition was the soil in which the folk revival — and Simon & Garfunkel — grew.
sources.author: AllMusic

influences.description: Odetta was the direct inspiration for Baez when she was starting out.

sources.citation: Odetta was my inspiration. When I first heard her, I knew what I wanted to do.
sources.author: Joan Baez

influences.description: Seeger introduced Baez to the world of folk and protest song at the dawn of her career.

sources.citation: Pete Seeger was there at the beginning for all of us. He showed us that music could change things.
sources.author: Joan Baez

influences.description: Guthrie is the foundation of the protest-song tradition that Baez continued and developed.

sources.citation: Woody Guthrie laid down the tradition of the protest song that I followed.
sources.author: Joan Baez

influences.description: Lead Belly was one of the first voices that opened Baez to the depth of folk music.

sources.citation: Lead Belly was one of the first voices I heard that told me folk music was something profound.
sources.author: Joan Baez

influences.description: Dylan was Donovan's primary inspiration at the start — he learned from him that you could be a poet in rock.

sources.citation: Bob Dylan was my first inspiration. I learnt from him that you could be a poet in rock.
sources.author: Donovan

influences.description: Like Dylan, Donovan drew on the Woody Guthrie folk tradition in his early work.

sources.citation: Like Dylan, Donovan drew on the Woody Guthrie folk tradition in his early work.
sources.author: AllMusic

influences.description: Pete Seeger's activist folk tradition was a major reference for Donovan's early work.

sources.citation: Pete Seeger's activist folk tradition was a major reference for Donovan's early work.
sources.author: AllMusic

influences.description: Shankar opened Donovan to Indian classical music after meeting through George Harrison and The Beatles.

sources.citation: Ravi Shankar opened up a whole new musical world for me. I met him through George Harrison.
sources.author: Donovan

influences.description: Dylan was the poetic reference for Neil Young and Stephen Stills as lyricists.

sources.citation: Dylan showed us that lyrics could carry the weight of literature in a rock song.
sources.author: Neil Young

influences.description: The Byrds' folk-rock sound was the direct template for Buffalo Springfield.

sources.citation: The Byrds' folk-rock sound was the direct template for Buffalo Springfield.
sources.author: AllMusic

influences.description: The Beatles inspired Buffalo Springfield to be more serious and layered in their arrangements.

sources.citation: The Beatles made us want to be more serious about what we were doing.
sources.author: Stephen Stills

influences.description: Hank Williams's country tradition ran through Buffalo Springfield's DNA — especially Neil Young.

sources.citation: Hank Williams was always in the background of what we did — especially for me.
sources.author: Neil Young

influences.description: The Byrds made folk-rock by turning Dylan songs into electric hits — 'Mr. Tambourine Man'.

sources.citation: Dylan gave us the words. We gave them a beat and an electric sound.
sources.author: Roger McGuinn

influences.description: When The Byrds saw The Beatles on Ed Sullivan, they went electric.

sources.citation: When we saw The Beatles on Ed Sullivan, that was it. We went electric.
sources.author: Roger McGuinn

influences.description: Pete Seeger's 5-string banjo style is what Roger McGuinn adapted to the 12-string Rickenbacker.

sources.citation: Pete Seeger's 5-string banjo style is what I adapted to the 12-string Rickenbacker.
sources.author: Roger McGuinn

influences.description: Harrison's Indian instrumentation and the Beatles' studio experimentation influenced The Byrds' psychedelic turn.

sources.citation: George Harrison's use of Indian instrumentation and the Beatles' studio experimentation influenced The Byrds' psychedelic turn.
sources.author: AllMusic

influences.description: The Byrds grew out of the Woody Guthrie folk tradition before electrifying it.

sources.citation: The Byrds grew out of the Woody Guthrie folk tradition before electrifying it.
sources.author: AllMusic

influences.description: Formby was one of Harrison's earliest musical influences — his banjolele virtuosity sparked George's fascination with the fretboard.

sources.citation: George Formby was among Harrison's earliest musical influences, cited in multiple biographical accounts.
sources.author: Wikipedia

influences.description: Cab Calloway was cited by Harrison as an early influence alongside Django Reinhardt and Hoagy Carmichael.

sources.citation: Harrison's earliest musical influences included Cab Calloway, Django Reinhardt and Hoagy Carmichael.
sources.author: Wikipedia

influences.description: Django Reinhardt was one of Harrison's earliest musical influences — his guitar mastery was foundational.

sources.citation: Harrison's earliest musical influences included Django Reinhardt.
sources.author: Wikipedia

influences.description: Hoagy Carmichael was cited by Harrison as one of his earliest musical influences.

sources.citation: Harrison's earliest musical influences included Hoagy Carmichael.
sources.author: Wikipedia

influences.description: Carl Perkins became a significant influence on Harrison by the 1950s; The Beatles recorded four Perkins songs.

sources.citation: By the 1950s, Carl Perkins was a significant influence on Harrison.
sources.author: Wikipedia

influences.description: Lonnie Donegan was a significant influence on Harrison by the 1950s — skiffle's do-it-yourself ethic helped launch his musical career.

sources.citation: By the 1950s, Lonnie Donegan was a significant influence on Harrison.
sources.author: Wikipedia

influences.description: Hearing 'Heartbreak Hotel' from a nearby house while cycling was Harrison's epiphany — it was the moment he committed to rock and roll.

sources.citation: In early 1956, Harrison had an epiphany: while riding his bicycle, he heard Elvis Presley's 'Heartbreak Hotel' playing from a nearby house.
sources.author: Wikipedia

influences.description: 'The first person I ever saw playing a guitar was Slim Whitman' — Harrison's own words on the origin of his obsession with guitars.

sources.citation: The first person I ever saw playing a guitar was Slim Whitman, either a photo of him in a magazine or live on television. Guitars were definitely coming in.
sources.author: George Harrison

influences.description: Baez helped establish the San Francisco folk scene from which Jefferson Airplane grew.

sources.citation: Jefferson Airplane grew out of the San Francisco folk scene that Joan Baez helped establish.
sources.author: AllMusic

influences.description: The Byrds' electric folk-rock was the direct model for Jefferson Airplane's early sound.

sources.citation: The Byrds' electric folk-rock was the direct model for Jefferson Airplane's early sound.
sources.author: AllMusic

influences.description: Dylan's literary approach to lyrics was a key influence on Paul Kantner and Marty Balin's songwriting.

sources.citation: Bob Dylan's literary approach to lyrics was a key influence on Jefferson Airplane's songwriting.
sources.author: Britannica

influences.description: Coltrane inspired the improvisational, meditative approach Jefferson Airplane brought to long concert forms.

sources.citation: John Coltrane showed us that you could take a piece of music somewhere completely unexpected through improvisation.
sources.author: Paul Kantner

influences.description: Muddy Waters and Chicago blues were where Jerry Garcia's electric playing started.

sources.citation: Muddy Waters and the Chicago blues were what we started with. That's where the electric came from.
sources.author: Jerry Garcia

influences.description: Coltrane showed Garcia that improvisation could be simultaneously infinite and spiritual.

sources.citation: Coltrane showed us that improvisation could be a spiritual journey. That's what we tried to do on stage.
sources.author: Jerry Garcia

influences.description: Lead Belly's folk tradition informed the acoustic side of Grateful Dead's eclectic repertoire.

sources.citation: Lead Belly's folk tradition informed the acoustic side of Grateful Dead's eclectic repertoire.
sources.author: AllMusic

influences.description: Hank Williams's country tradition runs through Grateful Dead's country-rock excursions.

sources.citation: Hank Williams is always there somewhere in what we do. Country music is part of our DNA.
sources.author: Jerry Garcia

influences.description: Miles Davis was Garcia's model for fearless musical reinvention over a career.

sources.citation: Miles Davis never stopped reinventing himself. That gave us permission to do the same.
sources.author: Jerry Garcia

influences.description: Muddy Waters' blues is the foundation Fogerty built the swamp-rock sound upon.

sources.citation: Muddy Waters is at the root of everything I do. The blues is the foundation.
sources.author: John Fogerty

influences.description: Elvis was Fogerty's first rock'n'roll revelation, defining CCR's baseline energy.

sources.citation: When I first heard Elvis on the radio, that was the moment. I knew I had to play rock and roll.
sources.author: John Fogerty

influences.description: Little Richard was the model for raw, ferocious rock'n'roll energy in Fogerty's playing.

sources.citation: Little Richard was pure energy. That's what I wanted CCR to have — that raw power.
sources.author: John Fogerty

influences.description: Williams's country roots feed the swamp flavour and autobiographical directness of Fogerty's lyrics.

sources.citation: Hank Williams' country tradition runs through the swamp-rock sound and autobiographical lyrics of CCR.
sources.author: AllMusic

influences.description: John Lee Hooker is the source of the hypnotic rhythmic boogie groove at the heart of CCR.

sources.citation: John Lee Hooker's hypnotic boogie groove is at the heart of CCR's swamp-rock sound.
sources.author: AllMusic

influences.description: The Byrds' folk-rock jangle was a direct influence on Love's early sound.

sources.citation: The Byrds' folk-rock jangle was a direct influence on Love's early sound.
sources.author: AllMusic

influences.description: Rolling Stones' raw blues-rock energy influenced Love's garage-rock side.

sources.citation: The Rolling Stones' raw blues-rock energy influenced Love's garage-rock side.
sources.author: AllMusic

influences.description: Dylan's literary approach to songwriting was a key influence on Arthur Lee's complex lyrical style.

sources.citation: Bob Dylan's literary approach to songwriting was a key influence on Arthur Lee's complex lyrical style.
sources.author: AllMusic

influences.description: The Beatles' orchestral experimentation on Revolver and Sgt. Pepper directly influenced Forever Changes.

sources.citation: The Beatles' orchestral experimentation on Revolver and Sgt. Pepper directly influenced Forever Changes.
sources.author: Britannica

influences.description: La Monte Young taught John Cale drone aesthetics and minimalism, which Cale brought directly into the VU sound.

sources.citation: La Monte Young taught me that a drone could be the basis of an entire musical world.
sources.author: John Cale

influences.description: Lou Reed cited Muddy Waters as the basic language that VU transformed into something new.

sources.citation: Muddy Waters was the foundation. We just took the blues somewhere it had never been before.
sources.author: Lou Reed

influences.description: Coltrane inspired the avant-garde improvisational approach to sound and structure in VU.

sources.citation: John Coltrane gave us permission to be completely free with structure.
sources.author: Lou Reed

influences.description: Dylan showed Lou Reed that a rock song could carry literary weight.

sources.citation: Dylan proved that you could write real literature in the form of a pop song.
sources.author: Lou Reed

influences.description: The Beatles inspired The Zombies to make Odessey and Oracle as a complete artistic statement.

sources.citation: The Beatles made us want to make an album that was a complete artistic statement. That's what Odessey and Oracle was.
sources.author: Rod Argent

influences.description: Ray Charles was Rod Argent's piano hero — the soul touch he was always reaching for.

sources.citation: Ray Charles was my piano hero. His touch and soul were what I was always reaching for.
sources.author: Rod Argent

influences.description: Dylan influenced the lyrical seriousness evident in The Zombies' later work.

sources.citation: Bob Dylan's influence on the lyrical depth of The Zombies is evident in their later work.
sources.author: AllMusic

influences.description: Ray Charles was the soul foundation for Steve Marriott's vocal style.

sources.citation: Ray Charles was everything. That's where I got my soul from.
sources.author: Steve Marriott

influences.description: Muddy Waters and Chicago blues were at the root of Small Faces' R&B energy.

sources.citation: Muddy Waters and Chicago blues were at the root of Small Faces' R&B energy.
sources.author: AllMusic

influences.description: The Beatles inspired the psychedelic turn Small Faces took on Ogden's Nut Gone Flake.

sources.citation: The Beatles' Sgt. Pepper inspired Small Faces to create their own conceptual psychedelic statement, Ogden's Nut Gone Flake.
sources.author: AllMusic

influences.description: James Brown was Marriott's model for rhythmic command and using voice as a rhythm instrument.

sources.citation: James Brown showed me how to work a crowd and how to use your voice as a rhythm instrument.
sources.author: Steve Marriott

influences.description: Ray Charles was Winwood's greatest musical hero — the press called him 'the white Ray Charles'.

sources.citation: Ray Charles was my greatest musical hero growing up. His voice could break your heart.
sources.author: Steve Winwood

influences.description: Miles Davis inspired the jazz improvisational fluidity in Traffic's arrangements.

sources.citation: Miles Davis showed us that jazz and rock didn't have to be separate. Traffic was our attempt at that fusion.
sources.author: Steve Winwood

influences.description: Muddy Waters' blues tradition is the root from which Traffic's blues-rock grew.

sources.citation: Muddy Waters' blues tradition was the root from which Traffic's blues-rock grew.
sources.author: AllMusic

influences.description: Booker T. was Winwood's organ groove reference — the sound he was chasing.

sources.citation: Booker T. and the MGs were the groove reference. That organ sound was what I was chasing.
sources.author: Steve Winwood

influences.description: Stevie Wonder's multi-instrumental approach and genre synthesis influenced Winwood's vision for Traffic.

sources.citation: Stevie Wonder's multi-instrumental approach and genre synthesis influenced Steve Winwood's vision for Traffic.
sources.author: AllMusic

influences.description: 'A Whiter Shade of Pale' is directly built on Bach's Air on the G String — a documented, legally-settled derivation.

sources.citation: A Whiter Shade of Pale is directly based on Bach's Air on the G String and his Suite No. 3 in D major.
sources.author: Gary Brooker

influences.description: Ray Charles was the soul-vocal model Gary Brooker was always aiming for.

sources.citation: Ray Charles was the voice I was always aiming for — that mixture of gospel and blues.
sources.author: Gary Brooker

influences.description: The Beatles' Sgt. Pepper era directly inspired Procol Harum's ambitious studio approach.

sources.citation: The Beatles' Sgt. Pepper era directly inspired Procol Harum's ambitious studio approach.
sources.author: AllMusic

influences.description: Dylan's literary surrealism was the primary influence on Keith Reid's lyrics.

sources.citation: Bob Dylan's literary surrealism was the primary influence on Keith Reid's lyrics for Procol Harum.
sources.author: AllMusic

influences.description: Ray Charles was Steve Winwood's greatest influence — the press called him 'the white Ray Charles'.

sources.citation: Ray Charles was my greatest influence. When people called me the white Ray Charles, that was the highest compliment.
sources.author: Steve Winwood

influences.description: Muddy Waters and Chicago blues were the foundation of Spencer Davis Group's R&B sound.

sources.citation: Muddy Waters and Chicago blues were the foundation of Spencer Davis Group's R&B sound.
sources.author: AllMusic

influences.description: Otis Redding's raw soul expressiveness was a direct influence on Winwood's vocal approach.

sources.citation: Otis Redding's raw soul expressiveness was a direct influence on Steve Winwood's vocal approach.
sources.author: AllMusic

influences.description: The Everly Brothers were the blueprint for everything The Hollies did with harmonies.

sources.citation: The Everly Brothers were the blueprint for everything we did with harmonies.
sources.author: Graham Nash

influences.description: The Beatles raised the bar for craftsmanship that The Hollies were always trying to match.

sources.citation: The Beatles raised the bar for everyone. We were always trying to match their level of craftsmanship.
sources.author: Graham Nash

influences.description: Buddy Holly gave the band not just a sonic model but part of their name.

sources.citation: The Hollies took their name partly in tribute to Buddy Holly.
sources.author: Rock and Roll Hall of Fame

influences.description: The Monkees were explicitly conceived as an American answer to The Beatles for a TV series.

sources.citation: The Monkees were explicitly conceived as an American answer to The Beatles for a TV series.
sources.author: Columbia Screen Gems

influences.description: Dylan showed the Monkees members they could write about real things, not just love songs.

sources.citation: Bob Dylan showed us that we could write about real things, not just love songs.
sources.author: Mike Nesmith

influences.description: The Byrds' folk-rock directly influenced Mike Nesmith's country-rock songwriting within The Monkees.

sources.citation: The Byrds' folk-rock sound directly influenced Mike Nesmith's country-rock songwriting within The Monkees.
sources.author: AllMusic

influences.description: The Byrds invented the folk-rock that The Mamas & the Papas turned into supreme pop harmony.

sources.citation: The Byrds showed us how folk could be electric and still beautiful.
sources.author: John Phillips

influences.description: The Everly Brothers' harmony style was expanded to four voices by The Mamas & the Papas.

sources.citation: The Everly Brothers' harmony style was expanded to four voices by The Mamas & the Papas.
sources.author: AllMusic

influences.description: Dylan's songwriting craft was a primary influence on John Phillips' approach to lyrics.

sources.citation: Bob Dylan's songwriting craft was a primary influence on John Phillips' approach to lyrics.
sources.author: AllMusic

influences.description: Joan Baez paved the way for female voices in serious folk music, which Cass Elliot followed.

sources.citation: Joan Baez paved the way for female voices in serious folk music, which Cass Elliot followed.
sources.author: AllMusic

influences.description: Ray Charles was the primary vocal reference for Felix Cavaliere — that gospel-blues feeling.

sources.citation: Ray Charles was the voice I was always trying to channel. That gospel-blues feeling.
sources.author: Felix Cavaliere

influences.description: Otis Redding was the soul singer The Rascals measured themselves against.

sources.citation: Otis Redding was the soul singer we all measured ourselves against.
sources.author: Felix Cavaliere

influences.description: Solomon Burke was the immediate model for the powerful early soul sound of The Rascals.

sources.citation: Solomon Burke was the real deal — a big voice with the soul of a preacher.
sources.author: Felix Cavaliere

influences.description: Booker T. Jones was Cavaliere's organ hero — the Stax groove was everything.

sources.citation: Booker T. Jones was my organ hero. That Stax groove was everything.
sources.author: Felix Cavaliere

influences.description: The Turtles debuted with a cover of Dylan's 'It Ain't Me Babe' which launched their career.

sources.citation: The Turtles' debut single was a cover of Bob Dylan's It Ain't Me Babe, which launched their career.
sources.author: White Whale Records

influences.description: The Byrds' folk-rock jangle was the direct model for The Turtles' early sound.

sources.citation: The Byrds' folk-rock jangle was the direct model for The Turtles' early sound.
sources.author: AllMusic

influences.description: The Beach Boys' California pop harmonies were a key influence on The Turtles' sunshine pop sound.

sources.citation: The Beach Boys' California pop harmonies were a key influence on The Turtles' sunshine pop sound.
sources.author: AllMusic

influences.description: James Brown is the funk foundation that Sly Stone took and radically exploded into psychedelic soul.

sources.citation: James Brown is the foundation of everything I do. I took his groove and exploded it.
sources.author: Sly Stone

influences.description: Sam Cooke was Sly's model for independent Black music business and for crossing gospel into pop.

sources.citation: Sam Cooke showed that a Black artist could own his music and cross over without selling out.
sources.author: Sly Stone

influences.description: Ray Charles's synthesis of gospel and pop was a foundational influence on Sly Stone's musical vision.

sources.citation: Ray Charles' synthesis of gospel and pop was a foundational influence on Sly Stone's musical vision.
sources.author: AllMusic

influences.description: The Beatles' studio experimentation gave Sly Stone the freedom to experiment on There's a Riot Goin' On.

sources.citation: The Beatles' studio experimentation gave Sly Stone the freedom to experiment with There's a Riot Goin' On.
sources.author: Britannica

influences.description: Ray Charles was Stevie's idol and proof that blindness was no barrier — his primary model from childhood.

sources.citation: Stevie Wonder (Rolling Stone, 1972): 'Ray Charles was my idol. He proved to me that being blind was not a limitation in music.'
sources.author: Stevie Wonder

influences.description: Little Richard was the first rock'n'roll energy that struck Stevie as a child.

sources.citation: Stevie Wonder (Rolling Stone, 1972): 'Little Richard was the first artist who made me feel the pure joy of music.'
sources.author: Stevie Wonder

influences.description: Sam Cooke showed how gospel feeling could reach everyone — the crossover model Stevie followed throughout his career.

sources.citation: Stevie Wonder (Rolling Stone, 1972): 'Sam Cooke showed me how gospel feeling could reach everyone, not just the church.'
sources.author: Stevie Wonder

influences.description: Nat King Cole's elegance and warmth were the sophistication Stevie always reached for.

sources.citation: Stevie Wonder (Rolling Stone, 1974): 'Nat King Cole had something special — a warmth and sophistication I always tried to find.'
sources.author: Stevie Wonder

influences.description: Sam Cooke was Gaye's primary vocal model — the same sweetness, the same crossover from gospel to pop.

sources.citation: David Ritz, 'Divided Soul' (1985), quoting Marvin Gaye: 'Sam Cooke was my model. I wanted the same sweetness, the same ability to move between gospel and pop.'
sources.author: Marvin Gaye

influences.description: Nat Cole was Gaye's model for elegance and crossover vocal style in his early career.

sources.citation: David Ritz, 'Divided Soul' (1985), quoting Marvin Gaye: 'Nat Cole was elegance itself. That's what I was reaching for in my early career.'
sources.author: Marvin Gaye

influences.description: Jackie Wilson was Gaye's model for stage mastery and vocal virtuosity.

sources.citation: David Ritz, 'Divided Soul' (1985), quoting Marvin Gaye: 'Jackie Wilson was the greatest entertainer I ever saw. I spent years trying to move like him.'
sources.author: Marvin Gaye

influences.description: Mayfield proved that soul music could carry political weight — the direct impulse behind What's Going On.

sources.citation: Marvin Gaye (Rolling Stone, 1971): 'Curtis Mayfield proved that soul music could be political. That gave me the courage to make What's Going On.'
sources.author: Marvin Gaye

influences.description: Harvey Fuqua of The Moonglows was Gaye's first personal mentor and brought him to Motown.

sources.citation: Motown Records official history: Harvey Fuqua of The Moonglows was Marvin Gaye's first mentor and brought him to Motown Records.

influences.description: Clara Ward sang at Aretha's father's church — her spiritual expressiveness was Aretha's direct and earliest model.

sources.citation: Aretha Franklin, 'From These Roots' (1999): 'Clara Ward was my inspiration. She sang at my father's church and I knew then what I wanted to be.'
sources.author: Aretha Franklin

influences.description: Sam Cooke showed the way from the church to the world without losing soul — Aretha followed the same path.

sources.citation: Aretha Franklin, 'From These Roots' (1999): 'Sam Cooke showed me the way from the church to the world. He crossed over without losing his soul.'
sources.author: Aretha Franklin

influences.description: Billie Holiday was Aretha's model for phrasing and emotional depth beyond genre.

sources.citation: Aretha Franklin, 'From These Roots' (1999): 'Billie Holiday taught me about phrasing. She made every word feel like it came from somewhere real.'
sources.author: Aretha Franklin

influences.description: Ray Charles's synthesis of gospel and R&B was foundational for Aretha's entire career.

sources.citation: Aretha Franklin, 'From These Roots' (1999): 'Ray Charles was the one who showed me how to take gospel into rhythm and blues. That was my whole career.'
sources.author: Aretha Franklin

influences.description: Sam Cooke was Otis Redding's first and primary vocal model.

sources.citation: Otis Redding (Rolling Stone, 1967): 'Sam Cooke was the man. He was the first singer I really tried to sound like.'
sources.author: Otis Redding

influences.description: Little Richard was Redding's Georgia neighbour — his ferocious energy defined Otis's stage temperament.

sources.citation: Otis Redding (Rolling Stone, 1967): 'Little Richard was from Georgia just like me. His energy was what I wanted on stage.'
sources.author: Otis Redding

influences.description: Ray Charles was the gospel-blues foundation feeding Redding's Southern soul.

sources.citation: Otis Redding (Rolling Stone, 1967): 'Ray Charles is the foundation of Southern soul. Everything comes from what he did.'
sources.author: Otis Redding

influences.description: James Brown was the model for stage energy and rhythmic command over an audience.

sources.citation: Otis Redding (Rolling Stone, 1967): 'James Brown owns the stage like nobody else. I learned how to work a crowd from watching him.'
sources.author: Otis Redding

influences.description: The Impressions' refined group harmony and elegant aesthetic were key models for The Supremes.

sources.citation: AllMusic: The Impressions' refined group harmony and elegant aesthetic were a key influence on The Supremes' style.
sources.author: AllMusic

sources.citation: Grove Music: The Impressions' close-harmony approach directly influenced Motown female groups including The Supremes.
sources.author: Grove Music

influences.description: Sam Cooke demonstrated that Black artists could reach mainstream success without compromising their identity.

sources.citation: AllMusic: Sam Cooke demonstrated that Black artists could cross over to mainstream success without compromising their identity.
sources.author: AllMusic

influences.description: Billie Holiday was Diana Ross's primary study in female vocal mastery and artistic independence.

sources.citation: Diana Ross (Rolling Stone, 1993): 'Billie Holiday was the first female artist I truly studied. Her control and emotion were beyond anything.'
sources.author: Diana Ross

influences.description: Jackie Wilson was the model for virtuoso vocal performance and impeccable stage choreography.

sources.citation: Otis Williams, 'Temptations' (1988): 'Jackie Wilson was the greatest performer we ever saw. His moves and his voice were superhuman.'
sources.author: Otis Williams

influences.description: The Impressions were the model for male vocal group harmony for The Temptations.

sources.citation: Otis Williams, 'Temptations' (1988): 'The Impressions showed us how male voices could blend and still sound individual.'
sources.author: Otis Williams

influences.description: James Brown's funky psychedelic soul directly shaped The Temptations' later Norman Whitfield productions.

sources.citation: AllMusic: James Brown's funky, psychedelic soul directly influenced The Temptations' later work with Norman Whitfield.
sources.author: AllMusic

influences.description: Sam Cooke was the vocal authority Levi Stubbs measured himself against.

sources.citation: Levi Stubbs (Rolling Stone, 1990): 'Sam Cooke was the voice I measured myself against. Pure, powerful, honest.'
sources.author: Levi Stubbs

influences.description: Jackie Wilson was Stubbs's model for dramatic vocal expressiveness.

sources.citation: Levi Stubbs (Rolling Stone, 1990): 'Jackie Wilson could make you feel everything with just his voice. That's what I wanted to do.'
sources.author: Levi Stubbs

influences.description: The Impressions' tight quartet harmonies were a formative influence on The Four Tops.

sources.citation: AllMusic: The Impressions' tight quartet harmonies were a formative influence on The Four Tops.
sources.author: AllMusic

influences.description: Jackie Wilson was the first singer who made young Smokey want to sing.

sources.citation: Smokey Robinson, 'Smokey' (1989): 'Jackie Wilson was the first singer who made me want to sing. He was everything.'
sources.author: Smokey Robinson

influences.description: Sam Cooke was the model for being smooth and soulful simultaneously.

sources.citation: Smokey Robinson, 'Smokey' (1989): 'Sam Cooke showed me that you could be smooth and soulful at the same time.'
sources.author: Smokey Robinson

influences.description: The Impressions' conscious soul songwriting influenced Smokey Robinson's more introspective work.

sources.citation: AllMusic: The Impressions' conscious soul songwriting influenced Smokey Robinson's more introspective work.
sources.author: AllMusic

influences.description: Billie Holiday was Smokey's model for poetic depth — what he wanted to bring to the soul ballad.

sources.citation: Smokey Robinson (1965): 'Billie Holiday was a poet. That's what I wanted to be in my songs — a poet.'
sources.author: Smokey Robinson

influences.description: Ray Charles's organ-in-soul context was the foundation Booker T. Jones built his style on.

sources.citation: Booker T. Jones (Rolling Stone, 2011): 'Ray Charles was everything to me. His use of the organ in a soul context was what made me want to play.'
sources.author: Booker T. Jones

influences.description: Muddy Waters was the blues foundation of the Stax sound that the M.G.'s translated into instrumental groove.

sources.citation: Booker T. Jones (Rolling Stone, 2011): 'Muddy Waters was the blues foundation that everything at Stax was built on.'
sources.author: Booker T. Jones

influences.description: Guthrie was the spiritual father of American tradition that The Band reinterpreted on Music from Big Pink.

sources.citation: Robbie Robertson (Rolling Stone, 1978): 'Woody Guthrie was the spirit we were trying to tap into. That America that he sang about.'
sources.author: Robbie Robertson

influences.description: Muddy Waters was the blues root refracted through The Band's Canadian-American lens.

sources.citation: Robbie Robertson (Rolling Stone, 1978): 'Muddy Waters and the Chicago blues were always in the back of our minds.'
sources.author: Robbie Robertson

influences.description: The Band grew directly from Dylan's touring Hawks — a documented founding relationship.

sources.citation: Rock and Roll Hall of Fame: The Band evolved directly from Bob Dylan's touring group The Hawks in 1965–1966.

influences.description: Hank Williams's lonesome quality was always present in The Band's sound, per Levon Helm.

sources.citation: Levon Helm (Rolling Stone, 2004): 'Hank Williams was in our DNA. That lonesome quality in his music was something we always reached for.'
sources.author: Levon Helm

influences.description: Lead Belly represented the primal American folk music that The Band wanted to honour.

sources.citation: Robbie Robertson (Rolling Stone, 1978): 'Lead Belly represented a kind of primal American music that we wanted to honour.'
sources.author: Robbie Robertson

influences.description: Coltrane's sheets-of-sound technique and spiritual intensity shaped Santana's extended soloing and his concept of music as meditation.

sources.citation: Carlos Santana, 'The Universal Tone' (2014): 'Coltrane showed me that music could be a spiritual discipline. His sheets of sound were something I tried to translate to guitar.'
sources.author: Carlos Santana

influences.description: Miles Davis's modal approach and sonic minimalism guided Santana toward space and colour in his playing.

sources.citation: Carlos Santana, 'The Universal Tone' (2014): 'Miles showed me the beauty of space in music. What you don't play is as important as what you do.'
sources.author: Carlos Santana

influences.description: Coltrane's modal explorations gave Booker T. Jones the harmonic framework for the MG's signature organ grooves.

sources.citation: Booker T. Jones (Rolling Stone, 2011): 'Coltrane was a huge influence on me harmonically. Those modal scales he used became central to how I approached the organ.'
sources.author: Booker T. Jones

influences.description: B.B. King was the first guitarist Santana truly worshipped — tone, vibrato, expressiveness.

sources.citation: Carlos Santana, 'The Universal Tone' (2014): 'B.B. King was the first guitarist I truly worshipped. His tone could make you cry.'
sources.author: Carlos Santana

influences.description: Tito Puente gave Santana the Latin rhythms that became the heartbeat distinguishing his group from any other rock band.

sources.citation: Carlos Santana, 'The Universal Tone' (2014): 'Tito Puente gave me the Latin rhythms that became the heartbeat of everything I do.'
sources.author: Carlos Santana

influences.description: Lightnin' Hopkins was the raw Texas blues foundation from which Santana developed as a guitarist.

sources.citation: Carlos Santana, 'The Universal Tone' (2014): 'Lightnin' Hopkins was one of the first guitarists I learned from. That raw Texas blues.'
sources.author: Carlos Santana

influences.description: Little Richard was the original wild man of rock — MC5 took that energy to its political extreme.

sources.citation: Wayne Kramer (Rolling Stone, 2003): 'Little Richard was the original wild man. We just took that energy to its logical extreme.'
